ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers omelette as a generic egg dish. No specific chicken omelette code exists; closest defensible match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 180 g (1 plate), equivalent to a two-to-three egg omelette with a moderate portion of diced chicken, as commonly served in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker stalls and home kitchens.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Egg omelet or scrambled egg, with meat (SR Legacy ID 173424), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for whole egg cooked and cooked chicken breast
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact chicken omelette composite entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.sg/) or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for egg omelette with meat (FDC ID 173424) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for whole egg (cooked) and cooked chicken breast. The input record values (energy 177.78 kcal, protein 13.89 g, fat 12.22 g, carbohydrate 2.78 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 250 mg/100g is plausible for a lightly salted preparation. Cholesterol 222 mg/100g is consistent with egg-dominant dishes per USDA SR Legacy data.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283 — analogous estimate based on egg-based omelette with protein (GI ~50, low category)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken omelette in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 50 (low category) based on the closest analogous food: plain egg omelette with protein, which is an egg-dominant dish with negligible carbohydrate (2.78 g/100g). Egg-based dishes with protein are consistently classified as low-GI foods in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 x 5.0) / 100 = 2.5, where carbohydrate per 180g serving = 2.78 x 1.8 = 5.0 g. GL of 2.5 is negligible and consistent with a very low-carbohydrate food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 250 mg/100g; cholesterol 222 mg/100g from egg yolks
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving (180 g) and avoid adding extra salt during cooking, as the combined sodium from eggs and chicken already approaches 450 mg per serving; use unsaturated oils such as canola or olive oil and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.78 g/100g), high protein (13.89 g/100g), negligible sugar (0.56 g/100g), low GI (~50)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken omelette is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content, negligible sugar, and low glycemic index, which together support stable postprandial glucose levels; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid high-GI accompaniments such as white bread or white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 222 mg/100g from egg yolks; saturated fat 3.33 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit egg yolk intake to no more than 3–4 per week as per current dietary guidelines; one serving of chicken omelette (180 g) contributes approximately 400 mg dietary cholesterol, which approaches the upper threshold for high-risk individuals, and cooking oil selection should favour unsaturated fats to keep saturated fat intake low.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of strong anti-inflammatory nutrients; pan-frying may increase AGE formation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken omelette is not contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis but lacks positive anti-inflammatory factors such as omega-3 fatty acids or polyphenol-rich ingredients; use minimal oil at medium heat to reduce advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation, and individuals sensitive to nightshades should avoid green chilli additions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Omelette (鸡肉煎蛋卷; Telur Dadar Ayam) is a pan-fried egg dish widely consumed in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker culture, home kitchens, and Indian-influenced food stalls. It is prepared by beating whole eggs and combining them with diced or shredded cooked chicken, then pan-frying in a small amount of oil until the egg is set and lightly golden. Common additions include diced onion, green chilli, salt, and black pepper. The texture is soft and slightly crispy at the edges, with a savoury, protein-rich flavour profile. Nutritionally, chicken omelette is high in protein (approximately 13.9 g/100g) and moderate in fat (12.2 g/100g), with very low carbohydrate content (2.78 g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly meal plans. Cholesterol is elevated at approximately 222 mg/100g due to egg yolk content, which is a consideration for individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia. Sodium is moderate at 250 mg/100g. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared with permissible ingredients. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice or roti, or as a standalone protein component in a balanced meal. Clinically, it is a good protein source for muscle maintenance and satiety, and its low glycaemic impact (estimated GI ~50, GL ~2.5 per serving) makes it appropriate for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: Is chicken omelette good for diabetics?
How many calories in a chicken omelette?
Can I eat chicken omelette if I have high blood pressure?
鸡肉煎蛋卷 营养成分

confidence_reason: No exact chicken omelette composite entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for egg omelette with meat (FDC ID 173424) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore whole egg and cooked chicken data. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on egg-dominant low-carbohydrate dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the absence of an exact approved-source match for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Malaysian/Singaporean chicken omelette preparation are not available as a composite entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, HPB Singapore Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-17. All core macros (protein_g 13.89, fat_g 12.22, carbohydrate_g 2.78) are non-zero and sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy adapted composite. Glycemic index is set to 50 (low category) as an analogous estimate based on egg-dominant protein dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; GI measurement is not directly available for this specific dish. Glycemic load per serving (180g) = 2.5, calculated as (50 x 5.0g carbohydrate per serving) / 100. Input source URL https://foodo.sg/ was reviewed but no exact chicken omelette composite record was located; USDA FoodData Central used as primary nutrient reference. Ethnic tags in input (Noodle, Fried Rice, Roti Prata) appear to be erroneous category tags from the GrabFood data pipeline and have been excluded from the output cultural classification.
